primary_diagnosis.name: Partial-thickness thermal burns with suspected inhalation injury

pathophysiology_summary: A flash-fire burn to the face and anterior chest with immediate blistering represents partial-thickness thermal injury. The combination of a hoarse voice, throat tightness, and coughing up carbonaceous (sooty) material strongly suggests concomitant inhalation of hot gases and combustion products, causing thermal and chemical injury to the upper and lower airway. Airway oedema from inhalation injury can progress silently over several hours even while the patient can still speak normally, and may culminate in sudden complete obstruction. This combination mandates immediate emergency assessment for possible prophylactic airway protection (intubation) before swelling worsens, alongside standard burns first aid and fluid resuscitation planning for the >10% TBSA partial-thickness burn.

differential.diagnosis: Thermal burn with suspected inhalation injury
differential.evidence_basis: Facial/chest burn with hoarse voice and throat tightness immediately raises inhalation injury concern.

differential.diagnosis: Simple partial-thickness burn without airway involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Possible if throat symptoms were unrelated, but the mechanism and timing make this less likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Facial burns with any voice change should immediately raise inhalation injury concern regardless of current speaking ability.

differential.diagnosis: Thermal burn with confirmed likely inhalation injury
differential.evidence_basis: Carbonaceous sputum (DI-003) together with hoarseness (DI-002) makes inhalation injury highly likely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage, immediate emergency airway assessment must be recommended regardless of the patient's current ability to speak.

differential.diagnosis: Partial-thickness thermal burns with suspected inhalation injury
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring immediate emergency transport for airway assessment and burns/fluid resuscitation management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan that delays emergency transport because the patient can currently still speak is a critical safety failure.

red_flags.description: Hoarse voice and throat tightness after facial burn exposure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Early sign of upper airway thermal injury and impending oedema.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to complete airway obstruction over subsequent hours.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Strong indicator of lower airway/inhalation injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed prophylactic airway management leading to obstruction.

red_flags.description: Singed nasal hair and eyebrows, carbonaceous material in the oropharynx, mild expiratory wheeze on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from current ability to speak
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might deprioritise emergency escalation because the patient can still speak in full sentences, missing that inhalation injury oedema evolves over hours and initial ability to speak does not exclude a developing airway emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Visual inspection of the burn/TBSA estimation
Oxygen saturation and auscultation for wheeze
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance dispatch (Priority 1) for assessment and probable prophylactic airway management, plus burns/fluid resuscitation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassurance based on current normal speech risks missed recognition of a rapidly evolving airway emergency.
